diagnoses.diagnosis: Alcohol-Related Liver Disease (Alcoholic Hepatitis / Early Cirrhosis Pattern)
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Classic alcohol-related liver disease pattern: AST 112 U/L and ALT 88 U/L with AST:ALT ratio >1.5 (characteristic of alcoholic hepatitis vs. NAFLD). GGT critically elevated at 188 U/L — most sensitive marker for alcohol-induced liver damage. Total bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL and direct bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL elevated. Albumin 3.2 g/dL indicates reduced synthetic function. INR 1.2 with PT 13.8 sec indicates early coagulation impairment. Ferritin 485 ng/mL with transferrin saturation 69% reflects hepatic inflammation and iron dysregulation. Daily alcohol consumption of 4-6 beers and father who died of cirrhosis at 62 make this the primary diagnosis.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: hs-CRP 7.8 mg/L with ESR 38 mm/h, CRP 12.4 mg/L, fibrinogen 462 mg/dL, and IL-6 8.4 pg/mL indicate severe active inflammation. LDL 155 mg/dL with Pattern B (LDL peak size 19.8 Å), LDL particle number 1580, ApoB 142 mg/dL, and TC/HDL ratio 6.7 constitute an extremely atherogenic lipid profile. Triglycerides 265 mg/dL are markedly elevated. Untreated hypertension since 2018, daily alcohol, and heavy red meat diet compound cardiovascular risk.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Pre-Diabetes with Electrolyte Disturbances and Hypogonadism
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: HbA1c 6.1% and glucose 108 mg/dL meet pre-diabetes criteria. Potassium 3.4 mmol/L low, magnesium 1.6 mg/dL low, bicarbonate 21 mmol/L low — all consistent with poor diet and excessive alcohol. Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL and BUN 24 mg/dL suggest early renal stress. Testosterone total 248 ng/dL and free 7.8 pg/mL are below range; DHEA-S 98 µg/dL markedly low — consistent with chronic alcohol-related Leydig cell suppression and HPA axis dysregulation.

foods_to_eat.food: Eggs and lean poultry — chicken, turkey (huevos, pollo, pavo)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Low albumin (3.2 g/dL) signals protein deficiency impairing liver regeneration. Adequate protein provides amino acids for albumin synthesis and liver repair. Avoid raw eggs.
Betaine in beets reduces fatty liver. Cynarin in artichoke stimulates bile production and liver regeneration. Both are hepatoprotective and widely available across Latin America.

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: EPA/DHA reduce triglycerides (currently 265 mg/dL), lower hs-CRP, and improve LDL particle quality. Also provides complete protein for liver repair.

foods_to_eat.food: Lentils and black beans (lentejas, frijoles negros)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Low glycemic, high fiber legumes stabilize blood sugar and reduce LDL. Also provide potassium, magnesium, and plant protein for liver support.

foods_to_eat.food: Leafy greens — spinach, Swiss chard (espinaca, acelga)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: High potassium and magnesium to address your electrolyte deficiencies. Folate supports liver methylation.

foods_to_eat.food: Turmeric with black pepper (cúrcuma con pimienta negra)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Curcumin is hepatoprotective, reduces NF-kB driven inflammation (IL-6 8.4, CRP 12.4), and supports liver detoxification. One of the most studied compounds for liver disease.

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Glutathione precursors and monounsaturated fats support liver health and improve HDL (currently low at 36 mg/dL).

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Beta-glucan directly lowers LDL by binding bile acids. Also reduces blood sugar spikes.
Selenium is depleted by alcohol use and is essential for glutathione peroxidase activity — the liver's primary antioxidant defense enzyme.
Natural electrolyte source to address low potassium (3.4 mmol/L) and magnesium without added sugars.
Allicin activates liver enzymes involved in flushing out toxins. Also reduces LDL and blood pressure.

foods_to_eat.food: Cruciferous vegetables — broccoli, cauliflower, cooked (brócoli, coliflor cocidos)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Sulforaphane activates Nrf2, the master regulator of antioxidant and detoxification pathways in the liver.

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Polyphenols and anthocyanins reduce vascular inflammation and oxidized LDL. Low glycemic and anti-inflammatory.

foods_to_eat.food: Extra virgin olive oil (aceite de oliva extra virgen)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Oleocanthal has anti-inflammatory effects comparable to ibuprofen. Replaces harmful cooking fats in fried food diet.
EGCG reduces liver fat accumulation, lowers GGT, and reduces inflammatory cytokines. Studies show benefit in alcoholic liver disease.

foods_to_avoid.food: Alcohol — all forms (toda clase de alcohol)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Your GGT of 188 U/L, AST:ALT ratio >1, INR 1.2, and bilirubin 1.8 indicate active alcoholic hepatitis. Your father died of cirrhosis at 62. Continued alcohol use will progress this to cirrhosis and liver failure. Complete elimination is the single most important intervention.

foods_to_avoid.food: Red meat and processed meats (carnes rojas y embutidos)
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Saturated fat, nitrates, and iron in red meat worsen liver inflammation, raise LDL, and contribute to Pattern B lipids. Your ferritin is already elevated at 485 ng/mL.
Create AGEs and oxidized lipids that burden the already-stressed liver. Your diet of heavy fried food plus alcohol is a direct pathway to cirrhosis.

foods_to_avoid.food: High-sodium foods — canned foods, processed snacks (enlatados, botanas)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Sodium promotes fluid retention (ascites) which is a complication of liver disease. Also worsens your untreated hypertension.

foods_to_avoid.food: Refined carbohydrates — white bread, white rice (pan blanco, arroz blanco)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Rapidly spike blood sugar and triglycerides. With HbA1c 6.1%, further glucose spikes worsen pre-diabetes progression.

foods_to_avoid.food: High-iron foods — organ meats, shellfish (vísceras, mariscos)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Ferritin is elevated at 485 ng/mL with transferrin saturation 69% — iron overload worsens oxidative liver damage.
Fructose directly increases liver fat and triglycerides. Your triglycerides at 265 mg/dL are already markedly elevated.
Fructose is processed exclusively by the liver, creating fat accumulation and worsening hepatic damage in already-injured liver.
Sleep only 5-6 hours — poor sleep impairs liver regeneration. Afternoon caffeine disrupts already-inadequate sleep.

foods_to_avoid.food: Commercial sauces and condiments high in sodium (salsas envasadas, aderezos)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Sodium restriction is essential in liver disease to prevent fluid retention and to manage your untreated hypertension.

exercise_recommendations.duration: 20-30 min, build to 45 min over 6 weeks
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Physically demanding construction work does not provide cardiovascular benefit. Structured walking reduces liver fat, improves insulin sensitivity, and begins addressing cardiovascular risk.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: With untreated hypertension and possible liver disease, avoid high-intensity exercise until physician clears. If you feel severe fatigue, abdominal distension, or yellowing of skin, stop and seek medical care immediately.

exercise_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Pre-Diabetes with Electrolyte Disturbances and Hypogonadism
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Preserves muscle mass, improves insulin sensitivity, and partially supports testosterone — all significantly impaired by alcohol use and liver disease.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Avoid heavy straining exercises (Valsalva) given untreated hypertension. No heavy overhead pressing.

exercise_recommendations.exercise_type: Stress-reduction practice — breathing exercises, light yoga
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Cortisol and stress hormones worsen liver inflammation. Stress management supports HPA axis normalization, improves sleep, and reduces reliance on alcohol as a stress coping mechanism.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Silymarin inhibits hepatotoxin binding to liver cells, reduces oxidative damage, promotes liver cell regeneration, and reduces fibrosis. Most studied hepatoprotective supplement. Your GGT 188 and AST/ALT elevation make this a priority.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No drug interactions. Very safe. Does not interact with any current medications.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Cardo Mariano widely available in pharmacies throughout Latin America.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Precursor to glutathione, the liver's primary antioxidant depleted by alcohol. NAC directly replenishes hepatic glutathione, reducing oxidative damage. Also supports albumin synthesis.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No current medications. Take with food. Well tolerated.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: NAC (N-Acetilcisteína) available in pharmacies across Latin America.

supplement_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Severe Systemic Inflammation with Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Reduces triglycerides (currently 265 mg/dL), lowers hs-CRP (7.8 mg/L), IL-6 (8.4 pg/mL), and fibrinogen. Also hepatoprotective for alcoholic liver disease.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Widely available in pharmacies throughout Latin America.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Serum magnesium 1.6 mg/dL is below normal. Alcohol depletes magnesium chronically. Repletion supports insulin signaling, blood pressure regulation, and sleep quality.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No drug interactions. May cause soft stools at higher doses.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Magnesium Citrate in pharmacies; Glycinate in health food stores.

supplement_recommendations.dosage_range: One high-potency methylated B-complex daily (B1 50mg, B6 25mg, Folate 400mcg, B12 500mcg)
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Alcohol chronically depletes B vitamins — particularly B1 (thiamine), B6, folate, and B12 — essential for liver metabolism and nerve function. Methylated forms bypass impaired conversion common with alcohol use.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No drug interactions. Start at lower dose if nausea occurs.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Complejo B available in pharmacies; methylated versions at health food stores.

patient_summary: Your lab panel reviewed 68 markers across 5 panels, with 41 values outside normal range — indicating one of the most severe metabolic profiles reviewed. The dominant finding is alcohol-related liver disease: GGT at 188 U/L, AST:ALT ratio greater than 1 (classic alcoholic pattern), bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, and INR 1.2 point to active hepatocellular damage with beginning coagulation impairment. Your father died of cirrhosis at 62, and your current trajectory mirrors that pattern — but this is reversible if alcohol is eliminated now. Simultaneously, you have severe cardiovascular risk with Pattern B small-dense LDL, ApoB 142 mg/dL, triglycerides 265 mg/dL, and hs-CRP 7.8 mg/L, paired with untreated hypertension since 2018. Pre-diabetes (HbA1c 6.1%), below-normal testosterone (248 ng/dL), and multiple electrolyte deficiencies from alcohol-driven depletion complete the picture. **Complete alcohol elimination is the most critical single action** — it will begin reversing liver damage, improving testosterone, lowering triglycerides, and reducing blood pressure simultaneously. Starting milk thistle, NAC, and omega-3 supplementation alongside dietary overhaul is the next priority. Urgent physician evaluation for liver assessment and hypertension management is strongly recommended.

system_summaries.summary: WBC is elevated at 12.2 K/µL, consistent with systemic inflammation and possible alcohol-driven immune activation. Hemoglobin is normal at 14.8 g/dL. Neutrophil percentage is at the upper limit (72%), reflecting active inflammatory state.

Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL and BUN 24 mg/dL suggest early renal stress — monitor kidney function closely as liver disease and hypertension both impair kidneys. Potassium (3.4 mmol/L), magnesium (1.6 mg/dL), and bicarbonate (21 mmol/L) are all below normal — classic alcohol-induced electrolyte depletion. Trace protein on urinalysis warrants monitoring given hypertension and early kidney stress.

Your liver panel shows significant damage. AST 112 U/L with AST:ALT ratio >1 is the hallmark of alcoholic hepatitis. GGT 188 U/L is the most sensitive marker of alcohol-induced liver damage. Albumin 3.2 g/dL and INR 1.2 indicate compromised synthetic function. Ferritin 485 ng/mL with transferrin saturation 69% reflects hepatic iron dysregulation from inflammation. Hepatitis B and C are negative, confirming alcohol as the primary etiology. Complete alcohol cessation and hepatology evaluation are urgently required.
HbA1c 6.1% and glucose 108 mg/dL confirm pre-diabetes. Alcohol and poor diet are primary drivers. Dietary intervention and alcohol cessation will improve these markers significantly.

Severely atherogenic lipid profile: ApoB 142 mg/dL, LDL 155 mg/dL, LDL Pattern B (peak size 19.8 Å — small-dense particles), particle number 1580, triglycerides 265 mg/dL, TC/HDL ratio 6.7. This profile, combined with untreated hypertension and chronic inflammation, represents very high short-term cardiovascular risk. Physician evaluation for statin therapy and blood pressure treatment is urgently recommended.

All inflammatory markers are elevated: hs-CRP 7.8 mg/L, ESR 38 mm/h, CRP 12.4 mg/L, fibrinogen 462 mg/dL, IL-6 8.4 pg/mL. D-dimer 380 ng/mL is within normal range. This inflammatory burden both reflects and amplifies liver damage and cardiovascular risk.

Testosterone (total 248 ng/dL, free 7.8 pg/mL) and DHEA-S (98 µg/dL) are both below normal, consistent with alcohol-related Leydig cell suppression. Alcohol directly impairs testicular testosterone production. LH and FSH are normal, confirming the dysfunction is at the testicular level. Alcohol cessation and liver treatment will partially restore androgen levels over 3-6 months.
